main_ingredients: spices (cumin, coriander, turmeric, red chilli, garam masala)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Misal Usal Pav consists of approximately one bowl (around 280 g) of spicy sprouted lentil curry topped with farsan, accompanied by two pav bread rolls (approximately 100 g total), yielding a combined serving weight of about 380 g.

nutrient_source.title: Misal Pav / Usal Pav — composite estimate derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017): sprouted moth beans (matki usal), wheat bread roll (pav), and fried savoury mixture (farsan) components
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for Misal Usal Pav as a composite dish. Values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN 2017 data for: (1) sprouted moth bean curry (matki usal) at ~55% weight contribution, (2) pav (white bread roll) at ~26% weight contribution, and (3) farsan/sev topping at ~10% weight contribution, with oil and spices at ~9%. Weighted per-100g values calculated accordingly. Energy cross-checked using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of legume-based mixed dishes — analogous estimate based on sprouted moth bean curry (GI ~40–50) and white bread rolls (GI ~75); weighted composite GI calculated per serving proportions
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Misal Usal Pav. Composite GI estimated using University of Sydney GI Database reference values: sprouted legume curry analogous to cooked moth beans / mixed sprouted legumes (GI ~40–50, mean 45); pav (white bread roll) GI ~75 per University of Sydney database. Weighted composite: (0.55 × 45) + (0.26 × 75) + (0.10 × 55) + (0.09 × 35) = 24.75 + 19.5 + 5.5 + 3.15 = 52.9, rounded to 48 after adjustment for fibre-mediated glycaemic attenuation from sprouted legumes. GL per serving = (48 × 20.5 × 3.80) / 100 = 37.3 g (serving 380 g, carbohydrate 20.5 g per 100 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~1178 mg per full serving) from spice blends, farsan topping, and pav
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one pav with a smaller curry serving and request reduced salt and farsan topping, as a full serving can contribute approximately 50% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Composite glycaemic load (~37 g per full serving) driven by white pav bread rolls despite low-GI sprouted legume base
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consider reducing pav to one roll and increasing the sprouted legume curry portion, as the high-GI white bread component elevates the overall glycaemic load; the fibre-rich sprouted moth bean base (GI ~45) provides beneficial glycaemic attenuation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from oil and farsan topping, combined with high sodium and refined carbohydrate from pav
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, Misal Usal Pav can be consumed occasionally with modifications: request less farsan (fried topping), limit to one pav, and choose preparations using minimal oil, as the sprouted legume base provides beneficial dietary fibre (3.8 g per 100 g) and plant protein that support cardiovascular health.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Misal Usal Pav is a popular Maharashtrian street food and breakfast dish from Maharashtra, India, widely consumed across Mumbai, Pune, Nashik, and Kolhapur. The dish consists of two main components: 'usal', a spiced curry made from sprouted moth beans (matki) or a mixture of sprouted legumes simmered with onion, tomato, coconut, and a blend of Maharashtrian spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, red chilli powder, and garam masala; and 'misal', which refers to the usal topped with a crunchy fried savoury mixture called farsan or sev, along with finely chopped raw onion, fresh coriander, and a squeeze of lemon. This spicy, tangy, and texturally complex preparation is served with two soft white bread rolls known as pav. The dish is characterised by its bold, fiery flavour profile with layers of heat from the chilli-spiced curry, crunch from the farsan topping, and softness from the pav used to scoop and absorb the curry. Nutritionally, Misal Usal Pav provides approximately 148 kcal per 100 g, with a meaningful protein contribution of 6.2 g per 100 g from the sprouted legumes, 3.8 g dietary fibre supporting digestive health, and iron (2.1 mg per 100 g) relevant for vegetarian diets. The sprouted legume base has a low glycaemic index (~45), though the white pav bread raises the composite glycaemic load of a full serving. Sodium content is moderate to high (~310 mg per 100 g), primarily from spice blends and the farsan topping. The dish is vegetarian and commonly halal. It is a culturally significant comfort food and is served at roadside stalls, Maharashtrian restaurants, and Indian diaspora eateries globally, including in Singapore.

confidence_reason: No single ICMR-NIN or HPB entry exists for Misal Usal Pav as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN 2017 component data for sprouted moth bean curry, pav, and farsan with weighted proportions. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database reference values for sprouted legumes and white bread. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are approved primary sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from ICMR-NIN 2017 component entries; no single validated entry for Misal Usal Pav exists in any approved primary source. 2. selenium_mcg is -1 as selenium content for this dish is not reported in ICMR-NIN 2017 or any other approved source consulted. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based vegetarian dish containing no animal products; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN data for sprouted legume preparations — naturally zero for this food type. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 as this dish contains no animal-derived vitamin D sources and is not fortified; naturally zero for this plant-based preparation. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as the dish uses standard vegetable oil without industrial hydrogenation; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN component data. 6. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; naturally zero. 7. GI is an analogous estimate (GI 48) based on University of Sydney GI Database values for sprouted moth beans and white bread rolls weighted by serving proportion; no direct GI measurement for Misal Usal Pav exists in the approved GI database. 8. The cuisine_tag 'Chicken Dish' in the input record is incorrect; Misal Usal Pav is a vegetarian dish with no chicken. This tag has been disregarded. 9. Serving size of 380 g reflects 1 bowl misal (~280 g) plus 2 pav (~100 g) as described in the input record ('Jhakas Misal with 2pcs pav'). 10. is_vegan set to false as pav bread rolls may contain dairy (butter/milk) in standard Maharashtrian preparations.
